# The Importance of Crop Diversity for Food Security

by [Carolina](https://farmtogarden.ca/author/carolina/ "Posts by Carolina") | Sep 14, 2023 | [Agriculture](https://farmtogarden.ca/category/blog/agriculture/) | [0 comments](https://farmtogarden.ca/blog/agriculture/the-importance-of-crop-diversity-for-food-security/#respond)

![The Importance of Crop Diversity for Food Security](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)

In a world challenged by a growing population, changing climate, and environmental degradation, the role of crop diversity in ensuring global food security has never been more critical. The intricate web of life on Earth is sustained by the richness and variety of its species, and agriculture is no exception. In this blog post, we delve into the significance of crop diversity, tracing its historical roots, understanding its benefits, and exploring how it contributes to safeguarding the future of food production.

## Definition and Significance of Crop Diversity

Crop diversity refers to the range of different plant species and varieties cultivated for food and other agricultural purposes. At its core, crop diversity embodies genetic variability, ensuring that various plant traits are available to address different challenges. This diversity not only provides a safety net against potential crop failures but also allows for adaptation to changing environmental conditions.

## Historical Perspective on Crop Diversity

Throughout history, traditional farming practices embraced crop diversity. Indigenous communities cultivated a wide array of crops suited to their local environments. These practices ensured food security, as diverse crops were less vulnerable to pests, diseases, and changing weather patterns. Indigenous knowledge played a pivotal role in preserving these local varieties, passing down generations of agricultural wisdom.

## The Threat of Monoculture Agriculture

In contrast to traditional practices, modern agriculture has leaned heavily towards monoculture farming—cultivating vast expanses of a single crop. While this approach increases efficiency and simplifies management, it also exposes food production to significant risks. Monocultures are susceptible to diseases that can decimate entire crops, threatening food security on a global scale.

## Benefits of Crop Diversity

Diverse crops contribute to ecosystem stability and resilience. When multiple crops are grown together, they create a balanced environment that naturally limits the spread of pests and diseases. Additionally, diverse crops utilize nutrients more efficiently and reduce soil erosion, leading to healthier and more productive soils.

## Nutritional Benefits and Dietary Diversity

Crop diversity extends beyond agricultural fields to the dinner table, shaping human nutrition. Different crops provide an array of essential nutrients, vitamins, and minerals. A diet rich in diverse crops offers better nutritional coverage, addressing dietary deficiencies and promoting better overall health.

## Climate Change and Crop Diversity

As the global climate changes, crop diversity becomes a critical tool for adaptation. Diverse crop varieties have evolved to withstand various environmental conditions, including extreme temperatures, drought, and flooding. By planting a mix of adaptable crops, farmers can mitigate the risks posed by unpredictable climate patterns.

## Genetic Resources and Breeding Programs

Conserving genetic resources is crucial for maintaining crop diversity. Seed banks house a diverse collection of crop varieties that serve as genetic libraries for future generations. Breeding programs use these resources to develop new varieties with improved traits, enhancing resilience to pests, diseases, and changing climates.

## Indigenous Knowledge and Local Varieties

Indigenous communities have preserved local crop varieties through generations of selective breeding and adaptation. These varieties often possess unique traits that enable them to thrive in specific regions. Preserving indigenous knowledge and local crop diversity is essential for maintaining food security and promoting cultural heritage.

## Global Initiatives for Crop Diversity

International organizations, treaties, and seed banks are dedicated to preserving crop diversity. Initiatives such as the International Treaty on Plant Genetic Resources for Food and Agriculture aim to facilitate access to genetic resources while ensuring benefits for farmers and communities.

## Challenges and Solutions

Maintaining crop diversity faces challenges, including the influence of commercial agriculture and the erosion of traditional practices. To address these challenges, sustainable farming practices, policy support, and community-based initiatives are essential.

## Role of Education and Awareness

Educating farmers and communities about the importance of crop diversity is a cornerstone of preserving this vital resource. Raising awareness about the benefits of diverse crops and the risks of monoculture farming empowers individuals to make informed choices about their agricultural practices.

The importance of crop diversity for food security cannot be overstated. As we navigate a rapidly changing world, embracing the wisdom of traditional practices, the potential of genetic resources, and the power of education becomes paramount. By fostering crop diversity, we not only ensure the availability of nutritious food for all but also cultivate a more resilient and sustainable agricultural system. As stewards of the Earth, it’s our responsibility to nurture this diversity, empowering future generations to thrive in harmony with the planet’s natural rhythms.

FULL CONTENT:
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Lifestyle → Sustainability Directory](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Lifestyle-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![What Are the Benefits of Crop Diversity?](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/what-are-the-benefits-of-crop-diversity-question-image.webp)

# What Are the Benefits of Crop Diversity?

![The image presents a still life composition featuring three citrus fruits set against a soft pink backdrop, conveying a sense of natural freshness and minimalist aesthetic. A whole pomelo, characterized by its textured, pale rind, sits at the center, exuding organic appeal](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/fresh-organic-grapefruit-and-pomelo-citrus-fruits-showcase-sustainable-plant.jpg)
![A centrally positioned halved grapefruit dominates the frame against a muted rosy background which reflects subtly underneath highlighting mindful consumption. The grapefruit, precisely cut, reveals its internal structure of vibrant pink segments separated by white pith](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/freshly-cut-pink-grapefruit-half-exposing-juicy-segments-promoting-sustainable.jpg)

## Fundamentals

Imagine a world where our plates are filled with a vibrant array of colors, textures, and tastes, stemming from a multitude of different crops. This is the essence of **crop diversity**, a concept that goes far beyond just having a varied selection at the grocery store. It is about cultivating a wide range of plant species and varieties for food, feed, fiber, and fuel.

Why is this variety so important? Let’s begin with the basics.

At its heart, [crop diversity Meaning → Crop diversity refers to the variety of plant species utilized in agriculture, encompassing genetic differences within and between crop populations.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/crop-diversity/)  is nature’s insurance policy for our food systems. Think of it as a biological safety net. If we rely on only a few crop varieties, we become incredibly vulnerable. A single disease or pest outbreak could wipe out vast swathes of our food supply, leading to shortages and instability.

**Diversity provides resilience**, ensuring that if one crop fails, others will survive and continue to provide sustenance. This fundamental principle is crucial for global food security.

Consider the Irish Potato Famine in the 19th century. Ireland heavily depended on a single potato variety, the ‘Lumper’. When potato blight struck, the lack of diversity meant devastation. Millions starved or emigrated.

This historical tragedy serves as a stark reminder of the dangers of monoculture and the critical importance of diverse crops. **Learning from history** is essential to avoid repeating past mistakes and build more robust agricultural systems for the future.

![This image captures a serene and miniature earth ecosystem, focusing on a small, pale mushroom with a smooth cap and delicate gills, positioned centrally. It stands among two textured greyish brown stones, one to the left and one directly behind, adding a grounding element](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/delicate-earth-ecosystem-mushroom-growth-alongside-quartz-crystals-reflecting.jpg)

### Why Is Crop Diversity Essential?

The benefits of crop diversity are multifaceted and extend from the field to our plates, and even to our cultural identities. Let’s explore some key advantages:

In essence, crop diversity is not just about agriculture; it’s about building a more secure, healthy, sustainable, and culturally rich future. It is about recognizing the **inherent value of variety** in nature and applying this principle to our [food systems Meaning → Food systems represent the complex network encompassing all activities and resources involved in feeding a population → from production, processing, distribution, and retail, to consumption and waste management.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/food-systems/)  for the benefit of all.

![This image presents a close up view of a decaying wood log, showcasing a miniature ecosystem perfect for inspiring sustainable living practices. The log is adorned with patches of pale green lichen, exemplifying symbiotic relationships in nature and highlighting organic growth processes](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/earthy-sustainable-living-natural-wood-log-displaying-fungi-lichen-ecosystem.jpg)
![An eye level close up photograph showcases a mature mushroom, centrally composed against a softly blurred background transitioning from grey at the bottom to light pink towards the upper edge. The mushroom cap, in focus, presents a cracked, textured surface with off-white hues and brownish spots and protrusions, suggesting natural decay and decomposition](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/exploring-forest-fungi-a-natural-decomposer-and-vital-part-of-the-woodland.jpg)

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the fundamentals, we begin to appreciate the intricate web of benefits that crop diversity weaves into our lives and the environment. It’s not merely about avoiding famine; it’s about fostering **agroecological resilience**, enriching our diets in profound ways, and adapting to the escalating challenges of a world in flux. Crop diversity, viewed through an intermediate lens, reveals itself as a sophisticated strategy for sustainable living.

Consider the concept of **nutritional security**. It extends beyond simply having enough calories. It encompasses access to a balanced diet rich in micronutrients, essential for optimal health and cognitive function. Monoculture, the practice of growing single crop varieties over large areas, often prioritizes yield and market demands over nutritional value.

This can lead to “hidden hunger,” where individuals consume enough calories but lack vital nutrients. Crop diversity offers a powerful antidote. By cultivating a wide spectrum of crops, from nutrient-dense grains and legumes to vitamin-packed fruits and vegetables, we can create food systems that genuinely nourish populations.

Furthermore, the **environmental advantages** of crop diversity are increasingly recognized as critical for planetary health. [Diverse cropping systems Diverse cropping boosts farm resilience, soil health, and biodiversity, moving agriculture towards sustainability.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/question/why-should-farmers-embrace-diverse-cropping-systems/)  mimic natural ecosystems, promoting soil health through varied root systems and nutrient cycling. They can reduce the need for synthetic inputs. For example, intercropping, where different crops are grown together in the same field, can naturally suppress weeds and pests, reducing reliance on herbicides and pesticides.

Cover cropping, using specific crops to protect and improve soil between cash crops, enhances soil fertility and reduces erosion. These practices, rooted in crop diversity, are fundamental to agroecological farming, a pathway towards more sustainable and environmentally sound agriculture.

![A tranquil scene showcases a pink hued body of water, likely a lake, with various natural rock formations emerging from the surface. These structures exhibit diverse sizes and textures, some topped with white, potentially mineral or salt deposits, depicting a delicate ecosystem](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/pink-waters-and-natural-rock-formations-showing-tranquil-sustainable-ecosystem.jpg)

### How Does Crop Diversity Enhance Ecosystem Resilience?

Ecosystem resilience, the ability of an ecosystem to withstand shocks and disturbances, is significantly strengthened by crop diversity. This is not just about individual farms but the broader agricultural landscape and its interaction with natural ecosystems. Here’s how:

By embracing crop diversity, we move towards agricultural systems that are not only more productive and sustainable but also contribute to the health and resilience of the wider environment. It is a shift from a simplified, industrial model to a more **harmonious and ecologically integrated approach** to food production.

![A close up captures a weathered grey stone, possibly concrete, colonized by a vibrant pinkish-orange crustose lichen, emphasizing a micro urban ecosystem thriving on an inanimate surface. The lichen appears in clusters and individual formations, some larger, resembling miniature coral structures, demonstrating nature's resilience and ability to adapt even in urban settings](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/vibrant-crustose-lichen-bloom-on-stone-surface-showcasing-urban-ecosystem.jpg)
![A single mushroom with a textured, spotted cap and stalk takes center stage, growing from a weathered, decaying log. The mushroom's off-white to light brown hues contrast subtly with the gray and brown tones of the aged wood](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/fungi-flourishing-in-natural-decay-exemplifying-sustainable-life-cycle-and.jpg)

## Academic

Delving into the academic dimensions of crop diversity unveils its profound significance, extending beyond mere practical benefits to touch upon philosophical, psychological, and even ethical considerations related to human existence and sustainability. From this vantage point, crop diversity is not just an agricultural strategy; it is a reflection of humanity’s relationship with nature, a **microcosm of our values** and aspirations for a viable future.

Consider the psychological impact of food systems. Our connection to food is deeply intertwined with our sense of well-being, cultural identity, and even our understanding of purpose. Monotonous diets, often a consequence of simplified, monoculture-based food systems, can contribute to a sense of disconnection from nature and a diminished appreciation for the richness of the natural world.

Conversely, exposure to a diverse array of foods, rooted in diverse agricultural landscapes, can **foster a sense of wonder**, connection, and gratitude. This psychological dimension of crop diversity, while often overlooked, is crucial for cultivating a more holistic and sustainable relationship with food and the environment.

Furthermore, the ethical implications of crop diversity are increasingly relevant in a world facing unprecedented environmental and social challenges. **Food sovereignty**, the right of peoples to healthy and culturally appropriate food produced through ecologically sound and sustainable methods, is intrinsically linked to crop diversity. When food systems are dominated by a few corporations and rely on a narrow range of crops, control over food production shifts away from local communities and farmers.

Crop diversity, particularly when championed through initiatives like seed saving and community-supported agriculture, empowers local communities, preserves traditional knowledge, and promotes food systems that are more equitable and just. This ethical dimension underscores the need to move beyond purely economic or efficiency-driven perspectives and embrace a more values-based approach to agriculture and food.

![The image presents a close up view of two slender mushrooms sprouting from a textured, brown rock surface. The mushrooms, the focal point, exhibit delicate, pale caps and thin stems, their organic forms standing in gentle contrast to the rough stone](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/embracing-small-natural-fungi-growth-on-earthy-stone-promoting-a-sustainable.jpg)

### What Role Does Crop Diversity Play in Cultural and Social Resilience?

Cultural and social resilience, the ability of communities and societies to adapt and thrive in the face of change, is deeply enriched by crop diversity. This connection extends beyond food security to encompass cultural practices, social structures, and even intergenerational knowledge transfer. Let us examine how crop diversity acts as a bedrock for societal well-being:

Crop Diversity and Resilience

| Dimension of Resilience | Impact of Crop Diversity | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Cultural Resilience** | Preservation of traditional foods, culinary practices, and cultural identities linked to specific crops and varieties. | Indigenous communities maintaining heirloom maize varieties for traditional ceremonies and dishes. |
| **Social Resilience** | Strengthening of local food systems, farmer networks, and community-based seed saving initiatives, fostering social cohesion and collaboration. | Community seed banks that preserve local crop varieties and share seeds among farmers, enhancing collective resilience. |
| **Economic Resilience** | Diversification of income sources for farmers, reduced dependence on single commodity markets, and creation of local food economies. | Smallholder farmers growing diverse crops for local markets, reducing vulnerability to fluctuations in global commodity prices. |
| **Ecological Knowledge Resilience** | Maintenance and transmission of traditional ecological knowledge related to crop cultivation, adaptation, and use of diverse varieties. | Elder farmers passing down knowledge about drought-resistant varieties and traditional farming practices to younger generations. |

In this context, crop diversity becomes more than just a collection of plant species; it transforms into a **repository of cultural memory**, social capital, and ecological wisdom. Protecting and promoting crop diversity is therefore not merely an agricultural imperative, but a societal one, essential for building resilient and thriving communities in a rapidly changing world. It is about recognizing the interconnectedness of culture, society, and nature, and actively fostering systems that value diversity in all its forms.

URL: https://extension.unr.edu/publication.aspx?PubID=3816

SUMMARY:
Production of diverse crops on the same land can produce many agronomic benefits in pest management by breaking insect and disease cycles, reducing weeds and

FULL CONTENT:
## Quicklinks

**EXTENSION**

**COLLEGE ORGANIZATION**

**CABNR Departments**

**University Tools**

**University resources**

## Search

![University of Nevada, Reno Homepage](https://www.unr.edu/Assets/Icons/logos/university-logo.svg)

# Extension

![extension logo for printing](images/logos/Extension-Pos-Horiz-Blue.png)

# Crop Diversification

Diversifying the cropping system is already being practiced by agricultural producers to overcome crop production challenges, such as high land values, increased input costs, varying weather factors, increased demands for new products, etc. These challenges are already posing obstacles to generate more revenue per acre from traditional crops such as alfalfa (Northern Nevada Business Weekly Report, 2008). Thus, sustainability concerns have raised interest in crop diversification among agricultural producers, especially throughout the world.

Crop diversification means growing more than one crop in an area. Diversification can be accomplished by adding a new crop species or different variety, or by changing the cropping system currently in use. Commonly it can mean adding more crops into an existing rotation. Diversification can also be implemented to replace low-value commodities with high-value commodities, such as vegetables and fruits. It can also include an integration of crops and livestock, defined as mixed farming. Crop diversity encompasses several aspects, such as crop species diversity, varietal diversity within crop species, and genetic diversity within crop species. It is recognized as one of the most feasible, cost-effective, and rational ways of developing a resilient agricultural cropping system.

## Benefits of crop diversification

Through crop diversification, farming households can spread production and economic risk over a broader range of crops, thus reducing financial risks associated with unfavorable weather or market shocks. Growing diverse produce may also help financially by expanding the market potential. The inclusion of a variety of crops, in some areas, can lead to the development of new agriculturally based industries, improving the economic potential of a rural community. Additionally, diverse cropping systems generally provide more varied and healthier food for humans and livestock.

Production of diverse crops on the same land can produce many agronomic benefits in pest management by breaking insect and disease cycles, reducing weeds and soil erosion, and conserving soil moisture. The more diverse a farming system is with plants, animals and soil-borne organisms, the more varied the population of beneficial pest-fighting microbes in the soil. This approach may improve the economic picture of the farm by reducing production costs and increasing gross income. For example, the inclusion of legumes into rotation reduces spending on nitrogen fertilizer or adding crops (two or more) into the rotation, resulting in fewer pest problems, reducing expenditures on pesticides.

Below is a list of some potential benefits of increasing crop diversity on the farm:

Some potential challenges to increased adoption of crop diversification on the farm are:

![4 maps of the U.S. comparing 1978, 1987, 1997 and 2012. Each map is color coded to reflect how many crops are grown in each area of the U.S. Nevada areas show between 0 and 4 different crops grown. ](https://naes.agnt.unr.edu/images/Publications/2021_9abc0feb-125e-4cd9-ab10-98794667efa1.jpg)

Figure 1: Crop species diversity as effective number of species in 1978, 1987, 1997 and 2012 on a county level basis for the contiguous U.S. (Adapted from Agullar et al., 2015). Black outlined areas focus on Nevada.

Although agriculture has been hugely successful at meeting the needs of an expanding world population, in terms of food, feed and fiber, agricultural intensification may have reduced crop biodiversity (Agullar et al., 2015). Figure 1 illustrates changes in crop diversity, over a multiple-year period, in the United States as a whole. However, in the Basin and Range areas comprising most of Nevada and parts of other western states, researchers found that there has been no significant change in crop diversity over multiple years. Within Nevada, 90% of cropland is in the production of alfalfa or grass hay, and not much crop diversity is found. The area outlined in black demonstrates changes in the species grown in Nevada over more than three decades. In three counties (Humboldt, Nye and Lincoln) out of a total of 17 Nevada counties, little change has been noticed over the years, as shown in Figure 1.

In Humboldt County, there is a decrease in the number of species grown from five to six species to three to four species for the 20-year period from 1978 to1997. Similarly, a reduction in crop species from three to four to one to two was observed in Nye County in the 10-year period from 1987 to1997. In contrast, an increase in the number of crops planted from one to two species to three to four species was found in Lincoln County over the 15-year period from 1997 to 2012. Not many changes were observed in crop diversity until 2012 in Nevada, as illustrated in Figure 1. Similar to the trend observed in Lincoln County, increased interest in alternative crops or adding variety to their already existing crops has been found in nearly every recent agricultural-related need assessment conducted by University of Nevada, Reno Extension faculty in various counties. This increased interest might be related to the rise in the number of challenges the crop producers face to raise profitable crops in Nevada, such as water rights, droughts, soil erosion, degraded soil quality, persistent pest problems, etc.

## Choosing diverse crops

Always look for opportunities to diversify your farm and markets. When selecting new crops, consider which crops are a good fit for your soils, climate, available equipment, irrigation and closeness to buyers. To increase the chances of success, a producer should always complete a thorough investigation of potential crops and markets before planting any new crop species.

The best source for determining if a new crop species has previously been grown in the area is to search online resources of the University of Nevada, Reno Extension or consult with local Extension personnel. Table 1 and Figures 2 and 3 show selected crops grown in Nevada over the last few years. It is always best to find other producers with experience growing crops you are considering. The producer is also advised to start on a small acreage with any new crop species before any large-scale production is planned. Producers must develop skills related to their production practices, management, pest control, harvesting and marketing of the new product.

Table 1: Selected crops tested and grown in Nevada.

| Group | Crops |
| --- | --- |
| Vegetables | Lettuce, onion, potato, mint, garlic, summer and winter squash, melon, tomato, amaranth, pepper, eggplant, radish, broccoli, peas, turnip, carrot, beet |
| Forage | Great Basin or Basin wild rye, barley, teff, corn, pearl millet, triticale, timothy, wheat |
| Grains/Seed | Barley, teff, amaranth, corn, soybean, dry bean, sorghum, chickpeas, spring, and winter canola |
| Fruits | Grape, prickly pear, Seaberry |

![Pearl millet plant being grown outside.](https://naes.agnt.unr.edu/images/Publications/2021_15329cb7-5d85-4b6e-a670-3ec129cde9bc.jpg)![Banana peppers surrounded by leaves on stem.](https://naes.agnt.unr.edu/images/Publications/2021_13125c0f-0db5-41cf-83df-f95592acbd04.jpg)

Figure 3: Sweet banana peppers grown in Lincoln County. (Adapted from University of Nevada, Reno Extension Special Publication 12-07).

## Conclusions

Diverse cropping systems tend to decrease the probability of widespread crop failures and pest pressure, and improve soil quality and crop yields. To support healthy, sustainable and profitable crop production in Nevada, producers should start looking for opportunities to add new crop species into their existing rotation. This addition will assist them in overcoming challenges presented by climate change, especially droughts and issues of water usage in Nevada as the demand for water increases. Additionally, changes in the policies that encourage crop diversity may be needed to support efforts to ensure community food security.

# Crop Diversification

Diversifying the cropping system is already being practiced by agricultural producers to overcome crop production challenges, such as high land values, increased input costs, varying weather factors, increased demands for new products, etc. These challenges are already posing obstacles to generate more revenue per acre from traditional crops such as alfalfa (Northern Nevada Business Weekly Report, 2008). Thus, sustainability concerns have raised interest in crop diversification among agricultural producers, especially throughout the world.

Crop diversification means growing more than one crop in an area. Diversification can be accomplished by adding a new crop species or different variety, or by changing the cropping system currently in use. Commonly it can mean adding more crops into an existing rotation. Diversification can also be implemented to replace low-value commodities with high-value commodities, such as vegetables and fruits. It can also include an integration of crops and livestock, defined as mixed farming. Crop diversity encompasses several aspects, such as crop species diversity, varietal diversity within crop species, and genetic diversity within crop species. It is recognized as one of the most feasible, cost-effective, and rational ways of developing a resilient agricultural cropping system.

## Benefits of crop diversification

Through crop diversification, farming households can spread production and economic risk over a broader range of crops, thus reducing financial risks associated with unfavorable weather or market shocks. Growing diverse produce may also help financially by expanding the market potential. The inclusion of a variety of crops, in some areas, can lead to the development of new agriculturally based industries, improving the economic potential of a rural community. Additionally, diverse cropping systems generally provide more varied and healthier food for humans and livestock.

Production of diverse crops on the same land can produce many agronomic benefits in pest management by breaking insect and disease cycles, reducing weeds and soil erosion, and conserving soil moisture. The more diverse a farming system is with plants, animals and soil-borne organisms, the more varied the population of beneficial pest-fighting microbes in the soil. This approach may improve the economic picture of the farm by reducing production costs and increasing gross income. For example, the inclusion of legumes into rotation reduces spending on nitrogen fertilizer or adding crops (two or more) into the rotation, resulting in fewer pest problems, reducing expenditures on pesticides.

Below is a list of some potential benefits of increasing crop diversity on the farm:

Some potential challenges to increased adoption of crop diversification on the farm are:

![4 maps of the U.S. comparing 1978, 1987, 1997 and 2012. Each map is color coded to reflect how many crops are grown in each area of the U.S. Nevada areas show between 0 and 4 different crops grown. ](https://naes.agnt.unr.edu/images/Publications/2021_9abc0feb-125e-4cd9-ab10-98794667efa1.jpg)

Figure 1: Crop species diversity as effective number of species in 1978, 1987, 1997 and 2012 on a county level basis for the contiguous U.S. (Adapted from Agullar et al., 2015). Black outlined areas focus on Nevada.

Although agriculture has been hugely successful at meeting the needs of an expanding world population, in terms of food, feed and fiber, agricultural intensification may have reduced crop biodiversity (Agullar et al., 2015). Figure 1 illustrates changes in crop diversity, over a multiple-year period, in the United States as a whole. However, in the Basin and Range areas comprising most of Nevada and parts of other western states, researchers found that there has been no significant change in crop diversity over multiple years. Within Nevada, 90% of cropland is in the production of alfalfa or grass hay, and not much crop diversity is found. The area outlined in black demonstrates changes in the species grown in Nevada over more than three decades. In three counties (Humboldt, Nye and Lincoln) out of a total of 17 Nevada counties, little change has been noticed over the years, as shown in Figure 1.

In Humboldt County, there is a decrease in the number of species grown from five to six species to three to four species for the 20-year period from 1978 to1997. Similarly, a reduction in crop species from three to four to one to two was observed in Nye County in the 10-year period from 1987 to1997. In contrast, an increase in the number of crops planted from one to two species to three to four species was found in Lincoln County over the 15-year period from 1997 to 2012. Not many changes were observed in crop diversity until 2012 in Nevada, as illustrated in Figure 1. Similar to the trend observed in Lincoln County, increased interest in alternative crops or adding variety to their already existing crops has been found in nearly every recent agricultural-related need assessment conducted by University of Nevada, Reno Extension faculty in various counties. This increased interest might be related to the rise in the number of challenges the crop producers face to raise profitable crops in Nevada, such as water rights, droughts, soil erosion, degraded soil quality, persistent pest problems, etc.

## Choosing diverse crops

Always look for opportunities to diversify your farm and markets. When selecting new crops, consider which crops are a good fit for your soils, climate, available equipment, irrigation and closeness to buyers. To increase the chances of success, a producer should always complete a thorough investigation of potential crops and markets before planting any new crop species.

The best source for determining if a new crop species has previously been grown in the area is to search online resources of the University of Nevada, Reno Extension or consult with local Extension personnel. Table 1 and Figures 2 and 3 show selected crops grown in Nevada over the last few years. It is always best to find other producers with experience growing crops you are considering. The producer is also advised to start on a small acreage with any new crop species before any large-scale production is planned. Producers must develop skills related to their production practices, management, pest control, harvesting and marketing of the new product.

Table 1: Selected crops tested and grown in Nevada.

| Group | Crops |
| --- | --- |
| Vegetables | Lettuce, onion, potato, mint, garlic, summer and winter squash, melon, tomato, amaranth, pepper, eggplant, radish, broccoli, peas, turnip, carrot, beet |
| Forage | Great Basin or Basin wild rye, barley, teff, corn, pearl millet, triticale, timothy, wheat |
| Grains/Seed | Barley, teff, amaranth, corn, soybean, dry bean, sorghum, chickpeas, spring, and winter canola |
| Fruits | Grape, prickly pear, Seaberry |

Figure 3: Sweet banana peppers grown in Lincoln County. (Adapted from University of Nevada, Reno Extension Special Publication 12-07).

## Conclusions

Diverse cropping systems tend to decrease the probability of widespread crop failures and pest pressure, and improve soil quality and crop yields. To support healthy, sustainable and profitable crop production in Nevada, producers should start looking for opportunities to add new crop species into their existing rotation. This addition will assist them in overcoming challenges presented by climate change, especially droughts and issues of water usage in Nevada as the demand for water increases. Additionally, changes in the policies that encourage crop diversity may be needed to support efforts to ensure community food security.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High crop diversity enhances ecosystem stability by reducing pest outbreaks and improving soil health, offering a natural defense against diseases and climate variability.
Integrating livestock into agro-ecosystems promotes nutrient cycling and pest management, improves soil fertility, and enhances the overall resilience of agricultural systems.
Local adaptation of crops through traditional practices ensures resilience to environmental changes, enriching genetic diversity and preserving cultural heritage in farming communities.
